Walker at 3657 Richmond Street, Jacksonville, Florida. Stokes replies that the parish's baptismal records do not start until 1798 and so contain no record of the baptism of Christopher Fitzsimons or Cashel Fitzsimons. Transcribes part of an old family record (from a monument in Croggan Church-yard, County Armagh, Ireland) erected to Owen O'Callaghan, recounting the O'Callaghan family's flight from Co. notes: A single page of a longer handwritten letter in blue ink, with no salutation, signature, or date visible. The writer reports family news: Nathalie Ferguson and her father going to Washington, Sam preparing to plant on Monday, Seaman expected to take Etta and Kit back, an upcoming visit to Imrie from Chattie, Rhett, and Cecile Inglesby, and a recent drive to Mr. Barnwell's place where spring had suddenly returned after a freeze.

people: Gen. Lee; Gen. Johnston; Mother
notes: A page of a longer handwritten letter in brown/sepia ink on aged paper. No salutation, signature, or date is visible. The writer comments on the bombardment of Vicksburg, the suspense of waiting for news of Lee and Johnston, and a published address to the people of Vicksburg. The writer then explains why she has not yet returned to Greenville: she stayed longer to be with her mother and the young ladies of the place.

The header reads 'Argyle / June' with year obscured. The writer apologizes for being dilatory in correspondence and explains that the letter was intended for the recipient's birthday; she sends wishes for many happy returns from herself, Miss Sonia, and Miss Ellen.

notes: A handwritten letter in brown ink on a single sheet, the conclusion of a letter signed 'C. P. FitzSimons.' At the foot, in a different hand, an annotation reads 'Elizabeth Porcher F.S. née Stoney &' / 'my grandmother Susan Barker F.S.' and 'Aunt Catherine Pinkin F.S. née Sidney F.' identifying the recipient and the writer. The letter discusses arrangements for the children, a planned trip to Charleston, and family news.

people: Aunt Ellen FitzSimons; Sally; Betty; Linda; Susy; Young Wm. Stiles; Sarabel; Mother
notes: Two facing pages of handwritten blue-ink letters. On the left, the conclusion of one letter, describing arrival at a plantation and the host family (Sally, Betty, Linda, Susy, young Wm. Stiles). On the right, the opening of a new letter from Aunt Ellen FitzSimons to her mother (annotated 'Aunt Ellen F. S. - to her mother - A. S. W.'), dated March 4th, recounting a comfortable journey to Atlanta and breakfast at the Hotel Aragon. The right-hand letter continues onto the next scan.

people: Aunt Ellen FitzSimons; Sarabel; Mr. Stiles; Sally; Mr. Wadley
notes: Continuation of a handwritten letter from Aunt Ellen FitzSimons to her mother, begun on the previous page (dated March 4th). Two facing leaves of the same letter pasted side by side, describing visits to a cemetery to see a Confederate monument, a green house full of plants, shopping in town, and a journey to New Orleans where they were met by a young Wadley.

people: John Rogers; William Rogers; Sarah Rogers; Abigail Rogers; Richard Reynolds Barlow; Joseph Sanford Barlow; Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Ellen Lillian Barlow; Samuel Barlow; Sherman Pinckney
notes: An album page with multiple items. At upper left, a handwritten 'Rogers' genealogy column. At upper center, a small inscribed paper labelling 'The account of the last illness of Henrietta Catherine Gaillard.' At upper right, a small studio photograph of a seated woman with pencilled annotations beside it. Three envelopes are pasted in a vertical stack down the right and middle of the page; in this scan all three appear closed. Additional handwritten text continues at the bottom of the leaf, mostly hidden by the lowest envelope. Page 046 is a second photograph of the same album leaf with the middle envelope open.

people: John Rogers; William Rogers; Sarah Rogers; Abigail Rogers; Richard Reynolds Barlow; Joseph Sanford Barlow; Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Ellen Lillian Barlow; Samuel Barlow; Sherman Pinckney; Susan Milliken Barker
notes: A second photograph of the same album leaf shown on page 045. The middle envelope (labelled 'Letter from Samuel Barlow') is now open, revealing a folded letter inside. With the middle envelope unfolded, more of the handwritten genealogy at the bottom of the leaf is now visible. The transcribable text on this leaf is recorded fully on page 045; the additional content visible in this scan is the bottom-of-leaf text and the inside of the open envelope.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Aunt Ellen; Father; Theodore
notes: A handwritten account of the last illness of Grandmother (Henrietta Catherine Gaillard, per the envelope on page 045), beginning with a dated entry for July 17th. Written in faded brown ink in a flowing cursive hand on lined paper. The narrative records her words to family members at her bedside. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Diana; Sarah; Aunt Betty; Ma; Father
notes: Continuation of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness from page 047. Records her farewells to the household servants, her words to family members, and her acceptance of religious comfort. The entry then advances to July 18th, when she rallied briefly. The page bears a small notation 'No John & [uncertain]' in the left margin. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Father; Aunt Ally
notes: Continuation of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness from pages 047–048. Records her account of a recurring dream or vision of the 'Dark River,' her quotation of the hymn 'Vital Spark,' and her reflections on the children of Aunt Ally. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Aunt Ally; Father; Aunt Gaillard
notes: Continuation of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness. Records her recollection of a childhood hymn ('Like Noah's weary Dove'), her thoughts of her own mother (referred to as Aunt Gaillard), and family reflections on her gentle, decisive character. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Cousin Esther; Cousin Robert Gourdin; Cousin Theodore Gourdin; Father; Rev. Mr. Drayton
notes: Continuation of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness. Records visits from Cousin Esther and the Gourdin cousins, her expressions of gratitude and her wish for her family's prayers, and the dated entry for Sunday 25th when she received the Rev. Mr. Drayton. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Rev. Mr. Drayton; Cousin Esther; Father
notes: Continuation of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness. Records the Rev. Mr. Drayton's words of comfort, her response to subsequent visits, her reference back to the 'Dark River' allegory, and the dated entry for Sunday 29th noting her continuing decline. Continues onto the next page.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Rev. Mr. Drayton
notes: Concluding page of the handwritten account of Grandmother's last illness, with the dated entry for Sunday 8th. Records her continuing decline, her patience under suffering, and Mr Drayton's interpretation of her prolonged life as a manifestation of grace. The lower half of the sheet is blank. Some red staining is visible at the lower edge of the sheet.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Joseph Stafford Barker; Samuel Barker; Ellen Milliken
notes: The outer face of a folded sheet of lined paper, bearing only a brief pencilled docket identifying the enclosed letter. The page is heavily creased from folding, with brown staining along the fold lines.

First page of a handwritten copy of a Civil War-era poem, with a prefatory note that the original was found on a soldier-volunteer who died in camp on the Potomac. Cursive in dark ink on a folded sheet, with deep horizontal fold creases. The poem continues onto the next page.

Second and concluding page of the handwritten poem begun on page 055. Cursive in dark ink on the verso of the folded sheet. The final stanza closes the poem with the picket's death. The last line 'And the life blood' appears with a struck-through preceding phrase.

people: Ellen; Theos
notes: Page 1 of a handwritten letter dated Charleston, October 9, 1851, addressed 'My Dear Child.' The writer reports a safe arrival home, references a servant or worker named Theos, and turns to an extended meditation on Christian duty, almsgiving, and benevolence. A small pencilled annotation near the salutation reads 'Two Sons of Paul / & Two daughters / of [Mrs?] Walker [uncertain]'. Letter continues onto the next page.

Page 2 of the handwritten letter begun on page 057. The writer continues the meditation on Christian duty, expanding on benevolence, almsgiving, and the example of Christ. The page breaks mid-sentence; the letter continues onto subsequent pages.

A handwritten letter (or letter fragment) on Christian almsgiving and benevolence, written in faded brown ink on a folded sheet. The hand is small and tightly spaced; ink bleed and creases make many words difficult. Continues onto page 060. Author and recipient not identified on this page.

Conclusion of the philosophical letter on Christian almsgiving begun on page 059. The closing line at the bottom of the sheet appears to bear a signature in pencilled annotation, very faint and partly obscured by an ink blot.

people: Ellen Milliken; Susan Milliken Barker; Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker); Sanford; Mr. Owens
notes: Page 1 of a handwritten letter dated 'Boston 21 Feb '58' (1858) from Ellen Milliken Barker to her daughter, opening 'My dear child.' A pencilled annotation at the top of the page in another hand identifies the correspondents: 'Ellen Milliken — Barker — & her daughter — Susan Barker FitzSimons (my grand mother) — A.F.W.' A circular embossed seal is impressed at upper left. The letter continues onto pages 062 and 063.

people: Ellen Milliken; Susan Milliken Barker; Mr. Bell; Mr. Bell's son-in-law; Mr. Bell's children; Mr. Bell's mother-in-law; Mr. Bell's daughter-in-law; Mr. Miles; Mrs. Macomba; Mr. Burnett; Miss Pithian; Miss Foulke; Christopher J. S.
notes: Page 2 of a handwritten letter from Ellen Milliken Barker to her daughter Susan Barker FitzSimons, 21 February 1858. Cursive in brown ink, with several names and quoted phrases. Densely written; many readings tentative. Continues from page 061 and onto page 063.

people: Ellen Milliken; Susan Milliken Barker; Mr. Miles; Theo; Sanford; Theodore; William; Ellen; Kate; Thomas; Kit; Christopher J. S.
notes: Final page of the handwritten letter from Ellen Milliken Barker to her daughter Susan Barker FitzSimons, 21 February 1858. The letter closes with a benediction and a small key at the foot identifying the people referred to in the body by initials (E. M. B. = Ellen Milliken Barker; Christopher J. S. as her son-in-law). Continues from pages 061 and 062.

people: Mother; Brother; Uncle Eddie
notes: A folded bifolium letter photographed showing two facing manuscript pages side-by-side. The left half holds the tail end of a previous page (with a vertical pencilled note running up the inner margin). The right half opens a fresh letter datelined 'Charleston Sept 27, 57' addressed 'My Dearest Child,' reporting on the writer's mother's grave illness, attended by 'Brother,' 'Uncle Eddie,' and others. The letter continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Mother; Brother
notes: Verso of the bifolium begun on page 064 — the continuation of the September 27, 1857 letter from Charleston. The writer reflects on the duty of friends in tending illness, on the spiritual support of human relationships, and on a relative who has 'redeemed the time' through pious conversation. Continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Albert Sidney Johnston
notes: Page 1 of a handwritten copy of an inscription titled 'In Memoriam' to Confederate General Albert Sidney Johnston (here spelled 'Johnstone'), who fell at Shiloh on April 6, 1862. The text is laid out in centered verse on a long sheet of lined paper. Continues onto pages 067–068; page 068 supplies the provenance — found pasted on a board at his New Orleans tomb, copied in Charleston, 25 February 1871.

people: Albert Sidney Johnston
notes: Page 2 of the handwritten copy of the 'In Memoriam' tribute to Albert Sidney Johnston, continuing from page 066. Contains the closing two stanzas of the verse. Page 068 holds the prose explanation of the inscription's origin and the date of copying.

people: Albert Sidney Johnston
places: New Orleans, LA; Texas; Charleston, SC
notes: Page 3 (final) of the handwritten copy of the 'In Memoriam' tribute to Albert Sidney Johnston begun on page 066. The copyist explains that the inscription was found pasted on a rough board at the General's tomb in New Orleans, before the removal of his remains to Texas, and that the author was never discovered. Closes with an instruction to 'ask the children all to memorize it' and a docket: Charleston, 25 February 1871.

people: Susan Milliken Barker; Stevens
notes: A handwritten letter on a folded sheet, written from Charleston February 24, 1855, addressed 'Dear Lyd' and signed 'Sam'l G. Barker'. The body advises the recipient on the trust provisions to be introduced into 'Stevens' Marriage Sett[lement]'. One short interlinear correction appears in the sixth trust clause.

people: Susan Milliken Barker; Lydia C. Bullard
notes: The reverse / address-fold side of the Barker letter on page 069. The sheet is otherwise blank; an address is written across the central fold in brown ink.

The first of two cross-hatched (over-written at right angles) handwritten letter sheets in brown ink. Dateline at upper right appears to read 'The Bowery'. Cross-writing makes most of the text very difficult to decipher; only fragments are confidently legible. A travel narrative — boats, banks, a sick child carried by a 'white girl', heat, a wagon ride from a steamer landing — runs through the readable portions.

The second sheet of the cross-hatched (over-written at right angles) handwritten letter begun on page 071. Cross-writing makes the page largely illegible; only scattered phrases can be read with confidence.

people: Mrs. Frank Hampton; Mama; Mr. Wade; Singleton; Mr. Wade; Pape; Brother
notes: A single typewritten page extracted (per the caption at upper left, 'From Life of Dr. W. S. Halstead') and reproducing a letter datelined 'From Millwood / Saturday evening / Dec. 29, 1855.' The letter describes a small dinner at Millwood of about fourteen, with affectionate reflections on 'the other day' (a recent meeting) and praise of the addressee's husband. A pencilled annotation at the foot identifies the recipient as 'Mrs. Frank Hampton' and notes 'from M. H. — / M —'.

people: Mrs. Walpole; Peter; Martha; Kate Stoney
notes: A page from the middle of a multi-page handwritten letter (the top continues from a previous scan, the bottom continues onto page 075). The writer describes household economy — buying candles and bright soap because gas is in town, weaving cloth, summer clothing for the boys, and noticing that her servant Peter is wearing her husband's stolen check shirts. Civil War-era references to 'Yankees' appear on the next page.

people: Peter; Clem
notes: The concluding page of the multi-page handwritten letter that begins on page 074. The writer describes how 'Peter' (a servant) had stolen shirts from her trunks, claiming the Yankees had distributed them, and explains why she did not confront him. She closes as 'Clem' wakes up. The reference to Yankees places the letter during or just after the Civil War.

people: Hugh Miller; Mr. Kingsley
notes: A handwritten extract or copy from Blackwood's [Magazine], August 1858, page 219, on the nature of intellect, genius, and the literary man, with reference to Hugh Miller (the Cromarty stonemason and geologist) and a remark by Mr. Kingsley. The page is torn at the right edge with some words lost; transcription continues onto page 077.

Continuation of the handwritten extract from Blackwood's [Magazine], August 1858 (begun on page 076). The argument moves from the literary 'Voice' to the dignity of the home and the daily work of women, before turning to a moral about teaching. Several lines at the top edge and bottom edge are partly lost or illegible.

Page 1 of a handwritten letter beginning 'My Dear Sir,' written in brown ink in a neat sloping hand. The writer thanks his correspondent for a letter pointing out something about Shelley's 'Prometheus,' praises 'Prometheus Unbound' as one of the most remarkable productions in any language, and recalls being puzzled in boyhood by the reflection of Christ in the Saturnian Titan. Letter continues onto the next page.

Page 2 of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 078, in the same brown ink and sloping hand. The writer continues his discussion of Shelley and the figure of Christ, and turns to Aeschylus. Letter continues onto the next page.

Page 3 of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 078, in the same brown ink and sloping hand. The writer continues his comparison of Shelley with Aeschylus and reflects on Shelley's scepticism. A vertical fold runs down the centre of the page. Letter continues onto the next page.

people: Susan Milliken Barker
notes: Concluding page of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 078. The writer closes his reflection on Shelley's *Prometheus Unbound* as an unintended concession to Christianity, thanks his correspondent for a suggestion, and signs off. The signature reads 'J.W. Miles' and the addressee at lower left is 'Samuel G. Barker Esqr.' A vertical fold runs down the centre of the page.

people: Susan Milliken Barker
notes: The outer panel of the folded-sheet letter (pages 078–081), showing the address written across the centre fold and a small red wax seal at the lower edge. A pencilled annotation in a later hand is interlined beneath the addressee's name. Several horizontal and vertical creases from folding are visible.

people: Saul; Ben
notes: Page 1 of a handwritten letter beginning 'Dear Saul,' written in brown ink in a hurried cursive. The writer reports that no letter has come acknowledging the salt boat, expects to want money for a sale tomorrow, lists what he sends and what is wanting (a balance of fillies and the heavy half of the stock boy's baggage), discusses whether to sell or divide some 'families,' and complains of the 'hot times' in the parish with candidates for and against [Bryan?]. Several words are uncertain owing to the rapid hand. Letter continues onto the next scan.

Concluding portion of a handwritten letter in brown ink, signed and dated 30th Jan'y 1857. The closing paragraph runs at the top of the sheet; below the signature is a tabulated list of orders with dollar amounts, totaling $1123.90. The lower two-thirds of the sheet are blank except for show-through of writing on the verso. Pronounced horizontal and vertical fold creases. Letter began on the previous scan.

people: Henry Stevens; Hennie; Mr. Thomas Ravenel; Mrs. Catherine Palmer; Eliza; Gendron; W. Henry Cain; Rose; Laura; Henry; Anna Maria Cain; Sam; Kate
places: Pinopolis, SC; Manassas; Richmond, VA; Bar Rows
notes: Page 1 of a multi-page handwritten letter, written in faded blue/grey ink on blue paper, dated Pinopolis, September 9th, 1862. The writer reports war news to a correspondent away from home: Henry Stevens has been wounded; Hennie has set out for Manassas with Thomas Ravenel, Mrs. Catherine Palmer, and Eliza to see the wounded Gendron. The writer worries about Hennie traveling so close to her confinement. Also mentions visiting Anna Maria Cain and the Bar Rows household. Several pencilled annotations run vertically along the top edge of the sheet. Letter continues onto the next scan.

people: Seaman; Lewis; Daddy Essex; Uncle Theodore Procher; Nina Ravenel; Hennie; Cous Rembert; Dr. Rembert; Ellen; Tody
places: Walhalla, SC; Charleston, SC; Whitehall; Pineville, SC; Aiken, SC; Charlottesville, VA; Richmond, VA
notes: Continuation of the handwritten letter begun on the previous scan, dated Pinopolis, September 9, 1862. The writer continues with personal news: hopes the recipient will not have to come to Charleston; reports that Father refuses to see the danger of an attack on Charleston; sends messages to Seaman and the boys; describes attending Uncle Theodore Procher's funeral; reports Nina Ravenel is in poor health; relays a letter from Cous Rembert at Charlottesville, Va.; closes with apologies for an uninteresting letter and notes weekly correspondence routine.

people: Tody; Mr. Pillow
notes: A torn and fragmentary handwritten letter mounted at the upper left of the page, with smaller torn fragments below at the right. Written in faded blue ink in a flowing cursive. The dateline reads '[Walhalla] July 16th 1862' (the place name is uncertain due to fading and cursive style). The writer reports forwarding war news by telegraph, describes a position two miles from a river opposite enemy forces protected by a creek and gun boats, reflects on Providence and Mr. Pillow, and praises the troops' resilience. Large portions of the sheet are torn away; only edge fragments remain at right and bottom. Letter continues onto the next scan.

people: Theodore; Tody; Capt. Sharpe; Mrs. Sharpe; Lizzie Hayer; W. Wayne; P. Wayne; Father; Mother; Kate; Uncle Sandford; Sister Tody
notes: Continuation of the torn letter from the previous scan, in the same hand and faded blue ink. Larger central portion of the sheet preserved than the previous scan, but the upper-right corner, the lower-left corner, and the right edge are torn away. The writer reports on family news: not receiving letters from home; correspondence with Theodore; carriage visits to and from the Sharpes; Father examining property to buy; Kate reading Dickens's 'Pickwick' and laughing at Sam Weller; Kate's attempt to start a letter to Sister Tody. Several lines at the bottom edge are torn away or only partially legible.

A nearly blank sheet of light-colored paper with a horizontal fold across the middle. Faint show-through of cursive writing on the reverse is visible at the top half. A short pencil note in cursive sits in the lower-left quadrant.

people: Elizabeth; Christopher; Catherine Ann; John Stoney; Peter Gaillard; Paul
notes: Printed letterhead reading 'LIME SPRING PLANTATION / EUTAWVILLE, SOUTH CAROLINA' at the top center. Below, in blue ink cursive, a heading 'Their children' with two columns labeled 'born' and 'died,' listing six children with birth and (where given) death dates. Five of the six names have a small check mark next to them. A personal note in the same hand follows below.

Page 1 of a heavily cross-written letter in brown ink. The primary horizontal text fills the full sheet, with a second layer of writing crossed perpendicularly over it, making most of the body extremely difficult to read. Datelined 'Walhalla 30 July' at upper right, beginning 'Dear Sir.' Only fragmentary phrases can be deciphered.

Continuation page of a heavily cross-written letter in brown ink, with a perpendicular second layer of writing covering the upper portion of the sheet. Most of the page is illegible. Scattered legible fragments mention 'Walter,' 'Martha,' 'Mrs. Vaughan,' 'the Narrative of Mrs. Mason,' 'Charleston,' and a wound in the arm.

A heavily cross-written letter in blue ink on light paper, with a perpendicular second layer of writing across the top. Large pinkish-red stains obscure portions of the lower half. Datelined 'Walhalla June' at upper right. Beginning is partly legible: 'Just see what a good little sister Old Theophy has [illegible].' References to Father, Lieut. Coleman, Frank Campbell, and Pomerie.

places: Charleston, SC; New Orleans, LA; Greenville, SC
notes: A handwritten letter page in brown ink, continuation of the letter begun on page 093 (Walhalla June). Heavy reddish stain covers the right side and lower right portion of the sheet. Mentions the Hampton Legion stopping at the hotel, Wheeler, a Madona [uncertain] taken prisoner and exchanged, news come to Charleston from New Orleans, Frank Campbell, Hunter, and Fannie suffering from a mild form of Scarlet Fever. Many words are illegible due to staining and faded ink.

A heavily cross-written handwritten letter sheet headed 'Charleston' with a partial date at the top. Two layers of writing at right angles to each other obscure most of the text. The hand is small and crowded; the vast majority of the body is illegible due to the overlapping cross-writing. Names and place references that can be glimpsed include Saunders, Naughty, Steven, Mary, Lynch, Greenville, Barber, Wheeler, Brazian, Henry, Sirona, Friday, Thursday, Davis, Yankees, Pat Sumter.

places: Argyle; Sullivan's Island, SC; Winnsboro, SC
notes: A handwritten letter page in brown ink, continuation from previous pages. Discusses news of various family and friends — mentions of Mary, Mrs. Mulberry [uncertain], Marian, Frank Campbell, Argyle on the 24th, Ellen to be married Wednesday 5th, Mr. King continues to decline, Margaret's little Mitchell, Tom Ferguson's Battery on Sullivan's Island, Mr. Walker's company, Mr. Ed White, the Yankees, Europe, May Captain [uncertain] sick & troubled, Winnsboro' boarding too expensive, Scarlet [Fever].

A heavily cross-written handwritten letter sheet headed 'Walhalla 30 June' (year not given on this page). Two layers of writing at right angles to each other obscure most of the text. Scattered legible fragments mention Mary, Theodore, the Ohlandus Galilee, Columbia, Carrie Evans, Pat Sumter's, Tennent[s], Mrs. King's [school?], Father's chamber, Louisa, and Maude. Heavy cross-writing prevents continuous transcription.

people: Henrietta Catherine Gaillard
places: New Orleans, LA; Flat Rock, NC; Greenville, SC; Columbia, SC
notes: A handwritten letter page in brown ink, continuation from previous pages. The writer reports the death of Henry, news of family members including Will, Ellen, Louisa, Frank, Marian, Hunter, the carrying of letters from New Orleans, taken from N.O. by an officer, escape to Flat Rock by a young Arthur Hayer, Cecelia Campbell's letters not received, Mr. Hampton, Theodore, fifteen months without seeing him, Mrs. Henry, Mrs. King's much better state since the news of Henry's death from Flat Rock or Greenville, Doctor Adele, and Kate.

A handwritten page in blue/grey ink in a sloping cursive hand, with no visible letterhead, salutation, or date — clearly an interior page of a longer letter. The writer recounts a buggy outing, a funeral, household details about cooking and the children's health, and a disappointing Sunday sermon by a German preacher at the Methodist church. Several spots and stains in the upper portion of the sheet. The page ends mid-sentence and continues onto the next scan.

A handwritten continuation page in the same sloping cursive and faded blue/grey ink as page 099, with no salutation or signature visible. The writer mentions Theodore and Aunt Bet, gives household news about borrowed clothes, blackberries, and Aunt Kate, and reflects on the mountain refuge. Letter continues from the previous page and onto the next scan.

people: William
notes: First page of a multi-page handwritten letter, headed 'Walhalla [date] June' at the top right and addressed 'Dear William.' The writer reports receiving 'the [North?] Dispatch' the previous day, news about the writer's father, plans about the landlord and lumber, and gives observations about the Walhalla village, nearby mountains, weather, and a journey from Pinehill to Walhalla. The letter continues onto the next scan.

people: Pierce Means; John Stratton; Mrs. Nelson; Mrs. Smith; Mrs. Pyatt; Mrs. Frazier; Lucretia Walker
notes: Continuation page of the Walhalla letter begun on page 101, in the same blue ink and sloping cursive. The writer describes the local people, Negroes, refugee families, the death of Pierce Means, the marriage and wounding of John Stratton, visits from Mrs. Nelson and her daughter, and the politeness of the Smith family, the Pyatts, and Mrs. Frazier. Letter continues onto the next scan.

people: Mrs. Crooks; Uncle Eden
notes: Continuation page of the Walhalla letter from pages 101–102, in the same blue ink and sloping cursive. The writer describes their lodgings (Tippan's Hotel after another house had been burned), the layout of the rooms, comments on the children's noise, longs for letters from home, and discusses an inquiry about returning by way of Pinebury or Whitehall. The page ends mid-sentence.

people: William; Thomas; E B
notes: Final leaf of the multi-page handwritten Walhalla letter that began on page 101 and continued through 102 and 103. Only the top quarter of the sheet bears writing; the rest is blank. The closing reads as a continuation from the previous scan ('letters to night — God protect you all from these wicked people'), is signed with the initials E B at the right, and bears a postscript instructing that the letter be sent to Thomas, who is to forward it to 'your Uncle' so that he may open it in case the recipient is absent.

people: Toby; Mother; Father; Kate; Connie
places: Richmond, VA; Charlotte, NC; Columbia, SC
notes: Page 1 of a multi-page handwritten letter dated Wednesday July 7th 1862. Written in dark ink in a small, dense cursive hand. Across the top of the sheet, a block of text running upside-down (the writer crossed an earlier page perpendicularly to save paper) is hard to decipher in this orientation. The body discusses telegrams from Toby of June 27th, 31st, and later, plus a Richmond despatch about a battle, and the writer's longing for the recipient to come visit. Letter continues onto subsequent scans.

people: Mrs. Barker; Mrs. Charles Stockheimer [uncertain]; Fanny Wigfall; Connie
notes: Page 2 of the multi-page handwritten letter dated July 7th 1862 (continuation from the previous scan). The page is severely obscured by ink bleed-through from the verso side, leaving most of the body text [illegible]. Only the top several lines and scattered words elsewhere can be made out. Fragments mention Walhalla, going to walk, Mrs. Barker, Mrs. Charles Stockheimer's [uncertain] baby, Fanny Wigfall, and a poultice on someone's foot. Letter continues onto subsequent scans.

people: Emma Lerray [uncertain]; Fanny Wigfall; Mary Smith; Eliza; Kate; Father; Mother; Miss Cox; Miss Bonneau; Toby; Connie; Uncle Eddie
notes: Page 3 of the multi-page handwritten letter dated July 7th 1862. Continues from the previous scan and runs onto the next. Describes Father's unexpected trip to Charleston, the daily routine in the household, mail arrangements, and a note in pencil from Toby dated 29th reporting that Connie's wound (between ankle and knee joint) involved 'the probable amputation of his lower limb.' A small caret-correction inserts the name 'Emma' above the line at the start of the page.

people: Uncle E.; Connie; Frank Pinckney [uncertain]; Fred Prioleau [uncertain]; Toby; Col. W.; Gabriel Hayne [uncertain]; Aunt Sally; Sam; Mother; Father; Fanny; Kate; Uncle John; W.; Bob; Jane
places: Richmond, VA; Vicksburg, MS; Charleston, SC
notes: Page 4 of the multi-page handwritten letter dated July 7th 1862. Continues from the previous scan. Reports on Connie's condition, the hot weather and conservative surgery, casualties (Frank Pinckney [uncertain], Fred Prioleau [uncertain], Gabriel Hayne [uncertain]), Col. W. being assigned to the 3rd Va. Regt. temporarily, news from camp, the fleet above Vicksburg, Uncle John going to bed every night with the clothes brush on account of red bugs, and arrangements for sharing the colonel's papers. Last visible line of the scan: 'I hope she is having a comfortable...'

people: Mrs. FitzSimons; Theodore; Uncle Sanford; Nell; Kate; Major Brown; Bell
places: Greenville; Rock Cliff; The Barrows; Columbia; Summerville; Pine Land; Augusta
notes: Page 1 of a multi-page handwritten letter, dated Greenville 30 Sep [1853?], in brown ink and a sloping hand on aged paper. The writer reports on the health of Mrs. FitzSimons and the baby, mentions the cold weather, work on a brown kid skin, travel plans to Summerville and the Pine Land, and a box sent by express to the Major from Augusta. Letter continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Laurie; May; Nell; Ellen Campbell; Mrs. Bingle Clarke; Marion; Minnie
notes: Page 2 of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 109, in the same brown ink and sloping hand. The writer continues with news of completing baby aprons, refugees, the closing of the Chief Hotel, lady [boarders?], and a small lot of land in Greenville bought by Nell. Letter continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Theodore; Marion; Martha; Major Brown
notes: Page 1 of a multi-page handwritten letter dated Charleston Sep 10th, in faded ink on paper with red staining/spotting throughout. The writer references a telegram from a 'Thos. 7th' at the Rapidan station, mentions silk for shirt fronts, hemstitches, and instructions about a bale of goods. Several passages are obscured by ink stains. Letter continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Theodore; Mrs. Lawton; Mrs. Theodore; Frank; Mary; Nancy
notes: Page 2 of the multi-page handwritten letter begun on page 111, in the same hand. The page is heavily stained with red ink/spotting that obscures large portions of the text. The writer mentions Mrs. Theodore Jones being sick of typhoid fever, news of various people, and at the foot of the page a page number '113'. Letter continues onto subsequent pages.

people: Peter Gaillard; Henrietta Catherine Gaillard; Rev. C. E. Gadsden
places: St. John's Parish, SC; Charleston, SC
notes: A small piece of aged paper with a marriage notice handwritten in pencil, evidently a transcription from The Courier of February 8, 1841. The paper is creased with multiple folds. The notice records the marriage of Peter Gaillard of St. John's Parish to Miss Henrietta C. Barker of Charleston, performed by the Right Rev. C. E. Gadsden.

people: Mrs. Theodore; Mr. Lawton; Ellen; Mary; Louisa; Gannie; Mr. Huger; Col. Rhett
notes: A handwritten letter page in brown ink, beginning mid-paragraph (no salutation visible). Signed in the lower right with what appears to be 'E.B.' Mentions illness, telegrams, and travel between places including Indian Spring, Bath, and Buncombe. Many words uncertain due to faded ink, paper damage, and a torn lower right corner.

people: Robt. McKay; Mr. Hampton; Joe Ferguson; Thomas; Susie
notes: Page 1 of a handwritten letter in brown ink dated at 'Greenville 14th July [63?].' Salutation reads 'Dear Sir.' At the very top a separate line notes the letter was sent 'to the Care of Robt. McKay Esq.' The writer reports anxious news from Charleston about the sickness of 'the Boys,' refers to telegrams, mentions Mr. Hampton, and notes that Joe Ferguson is ill of typhoid fever 'at the Barrows.' Many words uncertain due to faded ink and bleed-through.

people: Susan Milliken Barker; Ellen Milliken Barker; Christopher FitzSimons (2nd); Amy FitzSimons (Mrs. James Pickens Walker)
notes: The outer fold of a letter sheet, lined paper showing crease lines and a wax-seal stain at the upper right. A pencilled identifying note in the compiler's hand runs across the middle of the sheet. Faint show-through writing is visible toward the lower center. A small printed manufacturer's stamp (illegible) is in the lower right corner.

people: Susan Milliken Barker; Mrs. Barbour; Mary; Ellen
notes: A handwritten letter in pale ink on lined paper, addressed 'Dear Susan' and signed 'E.B.' at the close of the main paragraph. The mother writes from home about her interview with 'Mr. K,' her plans to take 'your G Mother' to visit Mrs. Barbour and Mary, urges Susan to come to her and her father the next day, and closes with affection. A short additional line at the bottom mentions taking care of someone (possibly 'Whitehall'). The lower right has a damaged/torn area; the lower left corner is also torn.

people: Theodore; Thomas; Mrs. Sam'l Heyward; Miss Rhett; Mary
notes: A handwritten letter fragment in brown ink on a small sheet of lined paper, addressed 'Dear Sis.' The writer reports both joy and sorrow, mentions enclosing a copy of 'Theodore's note in pencil,' grieves over Thomas's death, and notes that Mrs. Sam'l Heyward, her sister Miss Rhett, and Mary are staying in the same house and leaving the next day for Columbia. The right edge has an ink-bleed stain near the upper right; the page appears to be a partial sheet.
